query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the **Kea DHCP server** replaces the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) DHCP server. Kea DHCP is an implementation from ISC that includes fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers.

Key advantages of the Kea DHCP server include:
*   It is an extensible server solution with module hooks.
*   It allows reconfiguration through a REST API.

Sources:
- [10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Module and Plugin Improvements Ansible Core 2.20 includes a number of practical improvements to built-in modules and plugins: blockinfile and lineinfile module — A new encoding option has been added, enabling support for files in encodings other than UTF-8. This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. This improves visibility into the security context of files for customers in SELinux-enforcing environments. setup module (facts) — Added a new lvs subkey within each entry of ansible_facts['vgs'] to provide complete logical volume data scoped by volume group. The top-level lvs fact deduplicates logical volume names across volume groups and may be incomplete, making this new per-VG view essential for environments with complex LVM configurations. default callback plugin — A new option allows you to configure indentation for JSON and YAML output, giving you control over how task output is formatted for readability. deb822_repository module — Automatic installation of the python3-debian package when missing, reducing prerequisites for managing APT repositories on Debian-based target nodes. Removal of Deprecated Functionality Ansible Core 2.20 completes the removal of functionality that was deprecated in prior releases. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. This was previously deprecated in Ansible Core 2.16. The smart value historically selected between ssh and paramiko based on platform configuration. Customers should explicitly set their transport to ssh if not already doing so. Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the **Kea DHCP server** replaces the Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) DHCP server. Kea DHCP is an implementation from ISC that includes fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers.

Key advantages of the Kea DHCP server include:
*   It is an extensible server solution with module hooks.
*   It allows reconfiguration through a REST API.

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. Key advantages of the Kea DHCP server include:
*   It is an extensible server solution with module hooks.
*   It allows reconfiguration through a REST API.

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. Key advantages of the Kea DHCP server include:

*   It allows reconfiguration through a REST API.

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. This improves visibility into the security context of files for customers in SELinux-enforcing environments. setup module (facts) — Added a new lvs subkey within each entry of ansible_facts['vgs'] to provide complete logical volume data scoped by volume group. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. This was previously deprecated in Ansible Core 2.16. The smart value historically selected between ssh and paramiko based on platform configuration. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Module and Plugin Improvements Ansible Core 2.20 includes a number of practical improvements to built-in modules and plugins: blockinfile and lineinfile module — A new encoding option has been added, enabling support for files in encodings other than UTF-8. This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. This improves visibility into the security context of files for customers in SELinux-enforcing environments. setup module (facts) — Added a new lvs subkey within each entry of ansible_facts['vgs'] to provide complete logical volume data scoped by volume group. The top-level lvs fact deduplicates logical volume names across volume groups and may be incomplete, making this new per-VG view essential for environments with complex LVM configurations. default callback plugin — A new option allows you to configure indentation for JSON and YAML output, giving you control over how task output is formatted for readability. deb822_repository module — Automatic installation of the python3-debian package when missing, reducing prerequisites for managing APT repositories on Debian-based target nodes. Removal of Deprecated Functionality Ansible Core 2.20 completes the removal of functionality that was deprecated in prior releases. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. This was previously deprecated in Ansible Core 2.16. The smart value historically selected between ssh and paramiko based on platform configuration. Customers should explicitly set their transport to ssh if not already doing so. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. **Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: SMBv1 no longer installed with latest Microsoft Windows 10 and 2016 update (version 1709) Updated 8 Mar 2018 Contents Introduction Resolution Use Cases References Introduction Microsoft recently announced 1 that they will not install SMBv1 by default with the next Redstone 3 (version 1709) update for Windows 10 and Windows 2016. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Use Cases The following use-cases will no longer work on RHEL-6.x and RHEL-7.0/RHEL-7.1 systems: Linux as file sharing client Linux clients will no longer be able to access shares on a Microsoft server when SMBv1 has been removed or disabled. Content: SMBv1 no longer installed with latest Microsoft Windows 10 and 2016 update (version 1709) Updated 8 Mar 2018 Contents Introduction Resolution Use Cases References Introduction Microsoft recently announced 1 that they will not install SMBv1 by default with the next Redstone 3 (version 1709) update for Windows 10 and Windows 2016. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Use Cases The following use-cases will no longer work on RHEL-6.x and RHEL-7.0/RHEL-7.1 systems: Linux as file sharing client Linux clients will no longer be able to access shares on a Microsoft server when SMBv1 has been removed or disabled. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Use Cases The following use-cases will no longer work on RHEL-6.x and RHEL-7.0/RHEL-7.1 systems: Linux as file sharing client Linux clients will no longer be able to access shares on a Microsoft server when SMBv1 has been removed or disabled. Content: How to tell if a PCI device is supported on Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7, 8, or 9 Updated 4 Oct 2024 Notice RHEL6 provided a modules.pcimap catalog file that users could check to determine whether their devices were supported. This file is no longer available on RHEL 7 and later, but the modules.alias file can be a useful replacement. Caution! To determine if full support is available for a specific device, beyond just being recognized by the driver, check the set of release notes for the major version in question as to additional support qualifications. Under rare circumstances, some devices are included within drivers to allow unsupported recognition and use of specific devices for the convenience of our customers. For example, in RHEL 8.4 certain LSI storage controllers 1000:0071 was added back into the megaraid_sas driver, but per the prior Considerations in adopting RHEL 8, "11.1.2. Removed adapters" , these device is still unsupported. In addition, some devices recognized by a driver or some drivers themselves may only be in technical preview . Caution! As of 8.4 and later kernel versions, the modinfo alias pci output information for storage drivers is no longer sufficient by itself to determine specific device support status within the lpfc, qla*, megaraid_sas , mpt3sas , and aacraid drivers. ... These are output as a [VVVV:DDDD] hexadecimal value set when you use the lspci -nnnv command. The kernel uses that information to find a driver that supports that specific VID:DID pair. So the basic methodology to determine if a specific PCI card is recognized by a specific kernel version is a two step process: Method 1: Determine if driver has already been assigned Step 1: Use lspci -nnnvk and check card's output block for a driver: being present See " How to determine the make and model of HBA and version of associated driver from a RHEL Server? While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Content: Why does Leapp upgrade not prevent upgrades from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 when there are gfs2 Filesystems configured? Solution Unverified - Updated 24 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Pacemaker cluster Filesystem resource agents using fstype=gfs2 Issue Why does leapp upgrade not prevented when upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 when there are gfs2 Filesystems configured? Resolution Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 This issue is being tracked with the issue This content is not included. RHEL-64281 ( Create LEAPP actor for Pacemaker 9 -> 10 upgrades ) for RHEL 9. Root Cause When doing a leapp upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 there is no warning or upgraded is prevented from proceeding forward when there are pacemaker resources configured that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 or no longer included in RHEL 10 controld , Filesystem resources for gfs2 filesystems. The issue is that the leapp upgrade should either not continue with upgrade or provide some type of warning to the end user when it finds unsupported resources configured for pacemaker . The resources controld , Filesystems with fstype=gfs2 , or lvmlockd are no longer supported on RHEL 10. For more information see: Support Policies for RHEL Resilient Storage - RHEL High Availability Requirements for Resilient Storage Resilient Storage Add-On will be discontinued with RHEL 10 NOTE: The leapp upgrade should be successful, but once pacemaker is started on the RHEL 10 cluster nodes those resource-agents that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 will fail to start. Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Type: Solution

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

**Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Ansible Automation controller , This content is not included. Red Hat Satellite , or a RHEL 9 or RHEL 8 host). The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

The Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SAP Solution subscription provides support for RHEL System Roles for SAP with Ansible Core. Ansible Automation controller , This content is not included. Red Hat Satellite , or a RHEL 9 or RHEL 8 host). The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Kea DHCP is an implementation from Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) that provides fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers.

To install the Kea DHCP server in RHEL 10, you can use the `dnf` package manager:

```bash
sudo dnf install kea
```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server. Configuration details will typically involve editing configuration files, enabling and starting the `kea-dhcp4` and `kea-dhcp6` services (for IPv4 and IPv6 respectively), and configuring firewall rules.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Kea DHCP is an implementation from Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) that provides fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers.

To install the Kea DHCP server in RHEL 10, you can use the `dnf` package manager:

```bash
sudo dnf install kea
```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server. Configuration details will typically involve editing configuration files, enabling and starting the `kea-dhcp4` and `kea-dhcp6` services (for IPv4 and IPv6 respectively), and configuring firewall rules.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Kea DHCP is an implementation from Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) that provides fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers.

To install the Kea DHCP server in RHEL 10, you can use the `dnf` package manager:

```bash
sudo dnf install kea
```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server. Configuration details will typically involve editing configuration files, enabling and starting the `kea-dhcp4` and `kea-dhcp6` services (for IPv4 and IPv6 respectively), and configuring firewall rules.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Kea DHCP is an implementation from Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) that provides fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers.

To install the Kea DHCP server in RHEL 10, you can use the `dnf` package manager:

```bash
sudo dnf install kea
```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server. Configuration details will typically involve editing configuration files, enabling and starting the `kea-dhcp4` and `kea-dhcp6` services (for IPv4 and IPv6 respectively), and configuring firewall rules.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Kea DHCP is an implementation from Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) that provides fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers.

To install the Kea DHCP server in RHEL 10, you can use the `dnf` package manager:

```bash
sudo dnf install kea
```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server. Configuration details will typically involve editing configuration files, enabling and starting the `kea-dhcp4` and `kea-dhcp6` services (for IPv4 and IPv6 respectively), and configuring firewall rules.

query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, the ISC DHCP server has been removed and replaced by the Kea DHCP server. Kea DHCP is an implementation from Internet Systems Consortium (ISC) that provides fully functional DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers.

To install the Kea DHCP server in RHEL 10, you can use the `dnf` package manager:

```bash
sudo dnf install kea
```

After installation, you will need to configure the Kea DHCP server. Configuration details will typically involve editing configuration files, enabling and starting the `kea-dhcp4` and `kea-dhcp6` services (for IPv4 and IPv6 respectively), and configuring firewall rules.

query: How do I set up a DHCP server?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant, and I can certainly help you with RHEL-related questions.

In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and later, the ISC DHCP server has been deprecated. The recommended replacement for setting up a DHCP server is the Kea DHCP server.

To set up a Kea DHCP server on RHEL 9 or RHEL 10, you would generally follow these steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server package:**
    You will need to install the `kea` package.

    ```bash
    sudo dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    Kea's configuration is typically managed through JSON files. The main configuration file is usually located at `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` for IPv4. You will need to edit this file to define your subnets, IP address ranges, DNS servers, gateway, and other DHCP options.

    Here's a basic example of what a `kea-dhcp4.conf` might look like:

    ```json
    {
    "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
            "interfaces": [ "eth0" ]
        },
        "lease-database": {
            "type": "memfile",
            "persist": true,
            "name": "/var/lib/kea/dhcp4.leases"
        },
        "subnet4": [
            {
                "subnet": "192.168.1.0/24",
                "pools": [
                    {
                        "pool": "192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.200"
                    }
                ],
                "option-data": [
                    {
                        "name": "routers",
                        "data": "192.168.1.1"
                    },
                    {
                        "name": "domain-name-servers",
                        "data": "8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4"
                    },
                    {
                        "name": "domain-name",
                        "data": "example.com"
                    }
                ]
            }
        ]
    }
    }
    ```

    *   Replace `eth0` with the actual network interface your DHCP server will listen on.
    *   Adjust `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, `domain-name-servers`, and `domain-name` to match your network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring, enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service (for IPv4).

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    sudo systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Configure the firewall:**
    Allow DHCP traffic through your firewall. DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

    ```bash
    sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    sudo firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

5.  **Verify the service status:**
    Check if the Kea DHCP server is running correctly.

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

Remember to consult the official Red Hat documentation for the most up-to-date and detailed configuration instructions specific to your RHEL version.

Sources:
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.6 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html-single/9.6_release_notes/index)

Content: Samba Support in Red Hat Enterprise Linux Updated 20 Mar 2019 Supported setups in current RHEL releases with Samba 3.6 and later versions. General We don't support deprecated options if there are replacements for them. Customers should migrate to new options. If a customer is not able to migrate to a new option we should discuss the best way forward. Client Winbind and related tools (security=ads and security=domain) We support all winbind setups especially having a Linux client joined to an Active Directory domain. ... They are needed by Desktop Environments or Display Managers for logging in. Also for user login: libnss_winbind.so, libnss_wins.so and pam_winbind.so. Samba as a NT4 domain member for existing installations (security = domain) Server Deprecated security = share. Please move to security = user for current configurations. This feature is old and shouldn't be used anymore. It has been already removed in Samba 4.0. security = server. Please move to security = user for current configurations. It has been already removed in Samba 4.0. Supported File server security = user: File servers in 'security = user' mode are fully supported and will be in future. 'net rpc trustdom': For customers who already run a PDC we support trust relationships with AD. security = ads: We only support cases where winbind is running. smbd without winbind is unsupported. Security = domain: this should not be used in updated AD setups and should only be used for nt4 domains. CTDB+GFS2 setups are only supported as a standalone setup, 3rd party application accessing the filesystem is not supported. Print server We fully support Samba as a print server with cups and lprng backend. Primary Domain Controller (PDC)/Backup Domain Controller (BDC) (NT4 style) We support these configurations for all customers who already use it. These modes shouldn't be suggested to new customers as NT domains are a feature of Windows NT - which is already not supported by Microsoft. ... Red Hat Enterprise Linux is using the MIT Kerberos implementation as its Kerberos infrastructure of choice. The Samba build in Red Hat Enterprise Linux is using MIT Kerberos implementation in order to allow system-wide interoperability between both desktop and server applications running on the same machine.At the moment the Samba Active Directory Domain Controller implementation is not available with MIT Kerberos. -- The Red Hat Samba Team

query: How do I set up a DHCP server?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant, and I can certainly help you with RHEL-related questions.

In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and later, the ISC DHCP server has been deprecated. The recommended replacement for setting up a DHCP server is the Kea DHCP server.

To set up a Kea DHCP server on RHEL 9 or RHEL 10, you would generally follow these steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server package:**
    You will need to install the `kea` package.

    ```bash
    sudo dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    Kea's configuration is typically managed through JSON files. The main configuration file is usually located at `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` for IPv4. You will need to edit this file to define your subnets, IP address ranges, DNS servers, gateway, and other DHCP options.

    Here's a basic example of what a `kea-dhcp4.conf` might look like:

    ```json
    {
    "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
            "interfaces": [ "eth0" ]
        },
        "lease-database": {
            "type": "memfile",
            "persist": true,
            "name": "/var/lib/kea/dhcp4.leases"
        },
        "subnet4": [
            {
                "subnet": "192.168.1.0/24",
                "pools": [
                    {
                        "pool": "192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.200"
                    }
                ],
                "option-data": [
                    {
                        "name": "routers",
                        "data": "192.168.1.1"
                    },
                    {
                        "name": "domain-name-servers",
                        "data": "8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4"
                    },
                    {
                        "name": "domain-name",
                        "data": "example.com"
                    }
                ]
            }
        ]
    }
    }
    ```

    *   Replace `eth0` with the actual network interface your DHCP server will listen on.
    *   Adjust `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, `domain-name-servers`, and `domain-name` to match your network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring, enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service (for IPv4).

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    sudo systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Configure the firewall:**
    Allow DHCP traffic through your firewall. DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

    ```bash
    sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    sudo firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

5.  **Verify the service status:**
    Check if the Kea DHCP server is running correctly.

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

Remember to consult the official Red Hat documentation for the most up-to-date and detailed configuration instructions specific to your RHEL version.

Sources:
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.6 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html-single/9.6_release_notes/index)

query: How do I set up a DHCP server?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant, and I can certainly help you with RHEL-related questions.

In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and later, the ISC DHCP server has been deprecated. The recommended replacement for setting up a DHCP server is the Kea DHCP server.

To set up a Kea DHCP server on RHEL 9 or RHEL 10, you would generally follow these steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server package:**
    You will need to install the `kea` package.

    ```bash
    sudo dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    Kea's configuration is typically managed through JSON files. The main configuration file is usually located at `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` for IPv4. You will need to edit this file to define your subnets, IP address ranges, DNS servers, gateway, and other DHCP options.

    Here's a basic example of what a `kea-dhcp4.conf` might look like:

    ```json
    {
    "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
            "interfaces": [ "eth0" ]
        },
        "lease-database": {
            "type": "memfile",
            "persist": true,
            "name": "/var/lib/kea/dhcp4.leases"
        },
        "subnet4": [
            {
                "subnet": "192.168.1.0/24",
                "pools": [
                    {
                        "pool": "192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.200"
                    }
                ],
                "option-data": [
                    {
                        "name": "routers",
                        "data": "192.168.1.1"
                    },
                    {
                        "name": "domain-name-servers",
                        "data": "8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4"
                    },
                    {
                        "name": "domain-name",
                        "data": "example.com"
                    }
                ]
            }
        ]
    }
    }
    ```

    *   Replace `eth0` with the actual network interface your DHCP server will listen on.
    *   Adjust `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, `domain-name-servers`, and `domain-name` to match your network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring, enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service (for IPv4).

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    sudo systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Configure the firewall:**
    Allow DHCP traffic through your firewall. DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

    ```bash
    sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    sudo firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

5.  **Verify the service status:**
    Check if the Kea DHCP server is running correctly.

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

Remember to consult the official Red Hat documentation for the most up-to-date and detailed configuration instructions specific to your RHEL version.

Sources:
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.6 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html-single/9.6_release_notes/index)

query: How do I set up a DHCP server?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant, and I can certainly help you with RHEL-related questions.

In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and later, the ISC DHCP server has been deprecated. The recommended replacement for setting up a DHCP server is the Kea DHCP server.

To set up a Kea DHCP server on RHEL 9 or RHEL 10, you would generally follow these steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server package:**
    You will need to install the `kea` package.

    ```bash
    sudo dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    Kea's configuration is typically managed through JSON files. The main configuration file is usually located at `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` for IPv4. You will need to edit this file to define your subnets, IP address ranges, DNS servers, gateway, and other DHCP options.

    Here's a basic example of what a `kea-dhcp4.conf` might look like:

    ```json
    {
    "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
            "interfaces": [ "eth0" ]
        },
        "lease-database": {
            "type": "memfile",
            "persist": true,
            "name": "/var/lib/kea/dhcp4.leases"
        },
        "subnet4": [
            {
                "subnet": "192.168.1.0/24",
                "pools": [
                    {
                        "pool": "192.168.1.100 - 192.168.1.200"
                    }
                ],
                "option-data": [
                    {
                        "name": "routers",
                        "data": "192.168.1.1"
                    },
                    {
                        "name": "domain-name-servers",
                        "data": "8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4"
                    },
                    {
                        "name": "domain-name",
                        "data": "example.com"
                    }
                ]
            }
        ]
    }
    }
    ```

    *   Replace `eth0` with the actual network interface your DHCP server will listen on.
    *   Adjust `subnet`, `pools`, `routers`, `domain-name-servers`, and `domain-name` to match your network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring, enable and start the `kea-dhcp4` service (for IPv4).

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    sudo systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Configure the firewall:**
    Allow DHCP traffic through your firewall. DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

    ```bash
    sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=dhcp --permanent
    sudo firewall-cmd --reload
    ```

5.  **Verify the service status:**
    Check if the Kea DHCP server is running correctly.

    ```bash
    sudo systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

Remember to consult the official Red Hat documentation for the most up-to-date and detailed configuration instructions specific to your RHEL version.

Sources:
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.6 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html-single/9.6_release_notes/index)
